description: This figure plots the strain hardening rate (unit: GPa) as a function of true stress (unit: MPa) for 2Al steels (nominal composition Fe–30.5Mn–2.1Al–1.2C wt.%) under tensile deformation at an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Two samples with different annealing conditions are compared: the black curve corresponds to 2Al steel annealed at 450 °C for 1 h, while the red curve corresponds to 2Al steel annealed at 650 °C for 72 h. The strain hardening behavior is divided into two stages by a dashed boundary at ~1100 MPa true stress. In the first hardening stage, both samples exhibit a rapid decrease in strain hardening rate from ~5 GPa to ~2 GPa as true stress increases. In the second hardening stage, the 450 °C/1 h annealed sample maintains a relatively stable strain hardening rate of ~1.9 GPa up to high true stress, while the 650 °C/72 h annealed sample fractures at ~1150 MPa true stress without entering the stable second hardening stage. This result supports the conclusion that intergranular M3C-type carbides in the long-annealed 2Al steel interact with deformation twins, which are supposed to contribute to the second hardening stage, to trigger stress concentration and premature intergranular fracture, leading to limited uniform elongation.
